quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Ecological footprint"?

options.text: Liquid percolating through waste containing dissolved and suspended contaminants leaching to groundwater
Increasing concentration of toxins at successive trophic levels due to predation on contaminated prey
Average number of children per woman; replacement level is ~2.1 in developed countries, <2.0 in many developed countries
Land area required to support human activity sustainably, including production of resources and absorption of waste

quiz_questions.explanation: "Ecological footprint" is defined as: Land area required to support human activity sustainably, including production of resources and absorption of waste. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Planetary boundaries"?

options.text: Temperature increase from doubling atmospheric CO₂, estimated at 2.5–4°C based on paleoclimate and model evidence
Nine sustainability thresholds for earth system processes (climate, biodiversity, nutrient cycles, freshwater, land use, etc.) beyond which risk increases substantially
The largest harvest possible indefinitely without reducing stock below level supporting maximum reproduction
Agricultural clearing method where forest is cut and burned to create cultivated fields, a major deforestation driver in tropics

quiz_questions.explanation: "Planetary boundaries" is defined as: Nine sustainability thresholds for earth system processes (climate, biodiversity, nutrient cycles, freshwater, land use, etc.) beyond which risk increases substantially. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Peak resource"?

options.text: Point of maximum extraction rate followed by decline, already occurring for conventional oil and some mineral resources
Liquid percolating through waste containing dissolved and suspended contaminants leaching to groundwater
The downward movement of dissolved nutrients through soil with percolating water, potentially removing them from root zone
The restriction of a species to a particular geographic area, increasing vulnerability to local environmental changes and extinction

quiz_questions.explanation: "Peak resource" is defined as: Point of maximum extraction rate followed by decline, already occurring for conventional oil and some mineral resources. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Material footprint"?

options.text: Atmospheric gas (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, fluorine gases) absorbing infrared radiation and warming earth's surface
Harvesting fish faster than populations can reproduce, exceeding sustainable yield and causing population decline
Mass of material extracted to meet human consumption, greater than direct material input due to supply chain waste
The complete sequence of successional stages from pioneer community through intermediate stages to climax community in a given area

quiz_questions.explanation: "Material footprint" is defined as: Mass of material extracted to meet human consumption, greater than direct material input due to supply chain waste. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Linear economy"?

options.text: Site designed for solid waste disposal with clay or synthetic liners, daily cover, and leachate collection systems
Take-make-waste economy extracting resources, producing goods, and disposing waste, unsustainable with current consumption levels
Colorless gas produced from high-temperature combustion, later oxidized to nitrogen dioxide (NO₂)
A boundary between air masses with different temperature and humidity, driving precipitation and weather changes

quiz_questions.explanation: "Linear economy" is defined as: Take-make-waste economy extracting resources, producing goods, and disposing waste, unsustainable with current consumption levels. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Land area required to support human activity sustainably, including production of resources and absorption of waste"?

options.text: Photovoltaic cell
Habitat corridor
Ecological footprint
Porosity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Ecological footprint".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Nine sustainability thresholds for earth system processes (climate, biodiversity, nutrient cycles, freshwater, land use, etc.) beyond which risk increases substantially"?

options.text: Ventilation rate
Erosion
Planetary boundaries
Primary productivity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Planetary boundaries".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Point of maximum extraction rate followed by decline, already occurring for conventional oil and some mineral resources"?

options.text: Mass extinction
Halons
Secondary pollutant
Peak resource

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Peak resource".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Mass of material extracted to meet human consumption, greater than direct material input due to supply chain waste"?

options.text: Primary productivity
Bycatch
Material footprint
Habitat corridor

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Material footprint".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Take-make-waste economy extracting resources, producing goods, and disposing waste, unsustainable with current consumption levels"?

options.text: Umbrella species
Reproductive value
Linear economy
Grid integration

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Linear economy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Resource Depletion is accurate?

options.text: Resource Depletion describes how humanity is depleting renewable and non-renewable resources faster than they are replenished or regenerated, including fossil fuels, topsoil, freshwater aquifers, fisheries, and forests, creating scarcity and...
Mineral extraction generates massive waste streams—tailings and overburden—creating long-term contamination risks and habitat loss.

This unidirectional flow, combined with the second law of thermodynamics, explains why energy pyramids are always larger at the base and why ecosystems require constant solar input to maintain organization.
Population Age Structure describes the distribution of individuals across age classes, and fundamentally determines population growth rates, migration potential, and demographic projections.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Resource Depletion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Resource Depletion?

options.text: Loss of genetic diversity through habitat fragmentation, population bottlenecks, and inbreeding weakens species' long-term survival prospects and reduces the stability of entire ecosystems.
Nuclear plants produce 10% of global electricity with high reliability despite public concerns about catastrophic accidents (Chernobyl, Fukushima, Three Mile Island).

Resource depletion reflects overconsumption in wealthy nations (approximately 2.7 global hectares per person on average, but ~8 gha in the United States and other high-consumption nations) and accelerating extraction in developing countries.
The Montreal Protocol (1987), one of history's most successful environmental treaties, phased out ozone-depleting substances, allowing ozone layer recovery projected by 2070.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Resource Depletion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Resource Depletion?

options.text: Biomes are large-scale ecosystems classified by climate and dominant vegetation, each providing distinct services shaped by their physical and biological characteristics.
Poor indoor air quality contributes to respiratory disease, asthma, allergies, and sick building syndrome, creating significant health and economic costs.
Understanding weather patterns is essential for agriculture, disaster preparedness, and predicting climate change impacts.
Meeting demand within planetary boundaries requires reducing consumption, improving efficiency, and transitioning to regenerative resource use.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Resource Depletion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Resource Depletion?

options.text: Age structure shapes future population changes independent of current birth and death rates, making it crucial for long-term planning.
Sustainable fisheries management requires assessing stock status, setting science-based catch limits, and protecting critical habitats.
Energy efficiency improvements are constrained by rebound effects where lower energy costs increase demand, partially offsetting efficiency gains.

Resource Depletion describes how humanity is depleting renewable and non-renewable resources faster than they are replenished or regenerated, including fossil fuels, topsoil, freshwater aquifers, fisheries, and forests, creating scarcity and...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Resource Depletion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Resource Depletion?

options.text: Mineral extraction generates massive waste streams—tailings and overburden—creating long-term contamination risks and habitat loss.

Resource depletion reflects overconsumption in wealthy nations (approximately 2.7 global hectares per person on average, but ~8 gha in the United States and other high-consumption nations) and accelerating extraction in developing countries.

Ecosystem Services and Biomes provide tangible and intangible benefits to humans, including provisioning services (food, water, materials), regulating services (climate regulation, disease control), supporting services (nutrient cycling,...
Acid rain peaked in developed countries during the 1950s–1980s before emission controls, but remains a serious problem downwind of major industrial regions in Asia and eastern Europe.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Resource Depletion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Resource Depletion?

options.text: Accounting for material footprint of goods (mining waste, energy), prompting consumption reduction and circular economy transitions
Protecting coral reef ecosystems through MPAs that prevent fishing-related habitat destruction and allow reef fish recovery
Monitoring atmospheric CO₂ from fossil fuel combustion using ice cores and continuous monitoring to inform climate policy
Projecting human population growth using demographic transition models to plan infrastructure, food production, and resource allocation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Resource Depletion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Resource Depletion?

options.text: Protecting the Mediterranean Basin hotspot through transnational conservation agreements coordinating protection across Europe, Africa, and Asia
Monitoring freshwater aquifer depletion rates (Ogallala, Arabian, North China Plain) to prevent permanent loss of irrigation capacity
Creating wildlife corridors such as the Path of the Panther in Florida to connect fragmented habitats and enable panther population recovery
Treating municipal wastewater through primary (settling), secondary (biological), and tertiary (nutrient removal) steps to protect receiving waters

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Resource Depletion.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Resource Depletion applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Assessing seafood supply depletion through ecosystem models, restricting catch to sustainable levels and allowing stock recovery
Measuring mercury levels in fish tissue to establish consumption advisories protecting human health from mercury neurological impacts
Managing forest successional pathways after logging or fire by controlling which species regenerate to achieve desired forest composition
Managing atmospheric particulates through emission controls and clean air standards to improve visibility and reduce respiratory health impacts

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Resource Depletion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Resource Depletion?

options.text: Accounting for material footprint of goods (mining waste, energy), prompting consumption reduction and circular economy transitions
Reducing power plant SO₂ emissions through fuel switching to low-sulfur coal, coal desulfurization, or renewable energy transition

Using phytostabilization to immobilize heavy metals in soil (reducing bioavailability without plant uptake), or phytoextraction using hyperaccumulator plants that absorb and concentrate metals in harvestable biomass
Using spotted owls as umbrella species in Pacific Northwest forest conservation to protect old-growth forest ecosystems benefiting hundreds of species

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Resource Depletion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Resource Depletion in practice?

options.text: Installing mechanical ventilation with heat recovery in energy-efficient buildings to maintain air quality without energy losses
Establishing national parks and marine protected areas (like the Great Barrier Reef Marine Park) to maintain viable populations of multiple species
Projecting national population growth using age structure and fertility rates to guide development planning (infrastructure, education, healthcare)
Monitoring freshwater aquifer depletion rates (Ogallala, Arabian, North China Plain) to prevent permanent loss of irrigation capacity

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Resource Depletion.
